general_health__background.what_is_your_ancestry: Mixed European (primarily Irish and German) with some English and Scottish ancestry. Both of my parents were born in the Midwest in the United States, and our family has been in the U.S. for several generations. Culturally I identify as white American, but I grew up with a lot of Irish cultural influence on my mom’s side (St. Patrick’s Day celebrations, Irish soda bread, etc.) and German food traditions on my dad’s side (sauerkraut, sausages, potato dishes).
general_health__background.how_is_your_sleep: Generally fair but not great. I fall asleep relatively easily most nights, usually within 20–30 minutes, but I tend to wake up at least once or twice during the night. I often feel like my sleep is a bit lighter than I’d like, and I don’t always wake up feeling fully rested. I notice that if I look at my phone or laptop too close to bedtime, I have more trouble winding down and my mind keeps racing about work or family responsibilities. On weekends when I can sleep in a bit and don’t set an alarm, I feel more rested, which makes me think I’m not quite getting enough deep sleep during the workweek. I don’t nap regularly, but if I have a particularly bad night I might lie down for 20–30 minutes in the afternoon. I don’t snore loudly according to my partner, but I do toss and turn and sometimes wake up feeling stiff in my neck and shoulders.
general_health__background.why: I usually wake up once, sometimes twice, during the night. Often it’s to use the bathroom, especially if I’ve had tea or water too close to bedtime. Other times I wake up because my mind starts thinking about work projects or something that happened during the day—kind of a low-level anxiety or overthinking. Occasionally I wake up because my 9-year-old son has a nightmare or needs something, or because our cat jumps on the bed. When I wake up around 3–4 a.m., I sometimes have trouble falling back asleep for 20–30 minutes because I start scrolling on my phone or thinking about my to-do list for the next day. If I’ve had alcohol in the evening (even just a glass of wine), I notice that my sleep is more fragmented and I wake up more often and feel more restless.
general_health__background.any_pain_stiffness_or_swelling: I have mild but fairly consistent stiffness in my neck and upper back, which I think is largely from sitting at a computer most of the day for work. I try to stretch in the mornings, but if I skip it for a few days, I definitely feel more tightness and occasional tension headaches that start at the base of my skull and move up behind my eyes. I also have intermittent lower back discomfort, especially if I’ve been lifting my son’s sports equipment or doing yard work without paying attention to my posture. The pain is usually a dull ache rather than sharp, and it tends to improve with gentle movement, yoga, or a warm shower. I don’t have noticeable swelling in my joints, but my right knee sometimes feels a bit puffy after I’ve gone for a longer run or done a lot of stairs—no formal diagnosis, but I suspect some early wear and tear from years of recreational running. I don’t take daily pain meds, but I’ll occasionally take ibuprofen if I have a headache or if my back is particularly sore.
general_health__background.constipation_diarrhea_gas: My digestion is generally okay, but I do notice patterns. I tend toward mild constipation if I don’t drink enough water or if I eat a lot of bread, cheese, and processed foods for several days in a row. On those weeks, my bowel movements can be less frequent (every other day instead of daily) and a bit harder to pass. I also notice more bloating in the evenings when my fiber intake has been low. On the flip side, if I suddenly increase my fiber (for example, if I eat a big salad plus beans and whole grains in one day), I can get gassy and have looser stools the next morning. Dairy, especially regular milk and ice cream, can give me gas and mild cramping, so I tend to choose lactose-free milk or oat milk now. I rarely have true diarrhea unless I’ve eaten something that didn’t agree with me (like very greasy fast food or too much spicy food), and that might happen once every couple of months at most. Overall, I’d say I’m somewhat sensitive but manageable—when I pay attention to hydration and fiber balance, I feel noticeably better and more regular.
general_health__background.allergies_or_sensitivities_please_explai: I have seasonal allergies (mostly in the spring and early fall) that show up as sneezing, itchy eyes, and a runny or stuffy nose—likely due to tree pollen and ragweed. I take an over-the-counter antihistamine (cetirizine) when symptoms flare up. I’m not formally diagnosed with any food allergies, but I seem to have a mild lactose intolerance. When I drink regular cow’s milk or eat a lot of ice cream or soft cheeses, I get bloating, gas, and sometimes loose stools within a few hours. Hard cheeses and yogurt don’t bother me as much, and I tolerate lactose-free or plant-based milks (like oat or almond) much better. I also notice that very greasy or heavily fried foods make me feel sluggish and can upset my stomach for the rest of the day. No known drug allergies, and I’ve never had a serious anaphylactic reaction to anything.

medical_information.do_you_take_any_supplements_or_medicatio:
Current regular medications and supplements:
- Cetirizine 10 mg (generic Zyrtec): taken once daily as needed during allergy season (spring and early fall) for seasonal allergies.
- Vitamin D3 2000 IU: taken once daily in the morning with breakfast, recommended by my primary care physician after a slightly low vitamin D level on a routine blood test about two years ago.
- Magnesium glycinate 200 mg: taken most evenings about an hour before bed, mainly to help with muscle tension and to support sleep quality.
- Multivitamin (general adult formula): taken once daily with breakfast, fairly consistently for the last few years.
- Occasional ibuprofen 200–400 mg: taken as needed (maybe a few times per month) for headaches or muscle soreness, but not on a daily basis.
I do not take any prescription medications for chronic conditions, and I do not use hormonal birth control (my partner has had a vasectomy). I also occasionally drink herbal teas like chamomile or peppermint in the evening, but I don’t consider those formal supplements.
medical_information.any_healers_helpers_or_therapies_with_wh:
I currently see a licensed clinical social worker (therapist) once every two weeks via telehealth to work on stress management, emotional eating patterns, and general anxiety related to work and parenting. I’ve been in therapy with her for about 9 months.

I also see a massage therapist roughly once a month for a 60-minute session focused on neck, shoulders, and lower back tension. These sessions are not covered by insurance, so I schedule them when my budget allows, but I do notice a big difference in my muscle stiffness afterward.

I had a few sessions with a registered dietitian about a year ago to talk about intuitive eating and how to balance blood sugar and energy levels. I’m not currently meeting with her regularly, but I still refer back to the handouts and notes from those sessions.

Occasionally (maybe 3–4 times a year), I attend a restorative yoga class at a local studio, which feels almost like a therapeutic practice for my nervous system rather than a workout. I don’t currently work with any chiropractors, acupuncturists, or other alternative practitioners on an ongoing basis.
medical_information.what_role_do_sports_and_exercise_play_in:
Exercise is important to me both for physical health and mental well-being, but I’m not always as consistent as I’d like to be. I work a desk job and can spend 7–8 hours a day on the computer, so I try to build in movement to counteract that. My main forms of exercise are brisk walking, light jogging, and home-based strength workouts.

On a typical week, I aim for:
- Walking: 4–5 days per week, usually 30–40 minutes around my neighborhood or on a nearby trail. I often listen to podcasts or audiobooks while I walk, which makes it feel more enjoyable and helps me decompress after work.
- Light jogging: 1–2 times per week, especially when the weather is nice. I’m not training for races right now, but I like to do short jog-walk intervals (for example, 3 minutes jogging, 2 minutes walking) for about 25–30 minutes.
- Strength training: 2 times per week using dumbbells and bodyweight exercises at home. I follow short online videos (20–30 minutes) that focus on core, glutes, and upper body to support my posture and reduce back pain.
- Stretching/yoga: 10–15 minutes in the evening on 3–4 nights a week, mostly gentle stretching, basic yoga poses, and foam rolling to help with neck and shoulder stiffness.

Emotionally, I notice that when I’m moving regularly, I feel calmer, sleep better, and have fewer intense food cravings in the evening. When I fall out of my routine—like during busy work deadlines or when my son’s schedule gets hectic—I tend to feel more sluggish, more irritable, and more drawn to comfort foods and late-night snacking. I don’t consider myself an athlete, but I do see exercise as a key part of how I manage stress, maintain my weight, and feel more confident in my body.

food_information.what_foods_did_you_eat_often_as_a_child_:
As a child, breakfast was usually fairly quick and carb-heavy. On school days, I often had:
- Cold cereal with milk: things like Cheerios, Frosted Flakes, or Corn Flakes, usually with 2% cow’s milk. Sometimes I’d add sliced banana if my mom had time.
- Toast with peanut butter or butter and jam: white or wheat toast, often with grape jelly or strawberry jam.
- Instant oatmeal packets: especially the flavored ones like maple and brown sugar or apple cinnamon, made with milk in the microwave.
- Occasionally scrambled eggs and toast on weekends when my dad cooked, sometimes with bacon or sausage.
- Pop-Tarts or frozen waffles on rushed mornings: usually the frosted strawberry Pop-Tarts or Eggo waffles with syrup.
Beverages were usually a small glass of orange juice or apple juice, plus water. I didn’t drink coffee or tea as a child.

For lunch as a child, especially during elementary and middle school, I typically brought a packed lunch from home or ate school lunch a few times a week.

Common packed lunches:
- Sandwiches: peanut butter and jelly on white or wheat bread, turkey and cheese with mayo, or ham and cheese. Sometimes my mom would add lettuce and tomato, but often it was just meat, cheese, and condiments.
- Sides: a small bag of potato chips, pretzels, or Goldfish crackers; carrot sticks or celery with ranch dip occasionally; sometimes apple slices or a whole apple/banana.
- Snacks: granola bars (often the chewy chocolate chip kind), fruit snacks, or a small cookie.
- Yogurt cups: flavored yogurts like strawberry or vanilla, usually the sweetened kind.

When I had school lunch:
- Pizza day was my favorite (rectangular cheese pizza with a side salad or corn).
- Chicken nuggets or chicken patty sandwiches with tater tots.
- Spaghetti with meat sauce and garlic bread.
- Milk (usually chocolate milk if allowed, otherwise 2% white milk).

Overall it was pretty typical American kid food—lots of sandwiches, chips, and some fruit, not a lot of vegetables beyond the occasional carrot sticks or school salad.

Dinner growing up was usually a home-cooked meal, fairly traditional Midwestern style. Some of the most common dinners were:
- Spaghetti with meat sauce: ground beef in jarred tomato sauce, served over white pasta, with garlic bread and sometimes a side salad (mostly iceberg lettuce, shredded carrots, and bottled ranch dressing).
- Meat and potatoes: baked or grilled chicken breasts, pork chops, or meatloaf with mashed potatoes or baked potatoes, plus a vegetable like green beans, corn, or peas (often from a can or frozen).
- Tacos: ground beef seasoned from a packet, served in hard taco shells or flour tortillas with shredded cheese, lettuce, diced tomatoes, and sour cream. Sometimes we had Spanish rice or refried beans on the side.
- Casseroles: tuna noodle casserole, chicken and rice casserole, or tater tot hotdish, often made with cream-of-something soups.
- Pizza night: frozen pizza or takeout pizza once a week, usually on Fridays, with pepperoni, cheese, or veggie toppings.

We rarely ate out at restaurants when I was younger, maybe once or twice a month for fast food (McDonald’s or Burger King) or a family-style diner. Vegetables were usually simple and plain (steamed or microwaved), and we didn’t have many whole grains. Dessert was not every night, but we’d sometimes have ice cream, pudding cups, or homemade cookies.

As a child, my snacks tended to be pretty processed and sweet or salty. Some frequent options were:
- Crackers and chips: potato chips, cheese-flavored crackers, pretzels, and Goldfish.
- Cookies: Oreos, chocolate chip cookies, or whatever my mom had baked (often chocolate chip or oatmeal raisin).
- Fruit: apples, bananas, grapes, and sometimes oranges; these were available but I often chose the more “fun” packaged snacks instead.
- Granola bars: chewy chocolate chip or peanut butter granola bars.
- After-school snacks: microwave popcorn, string cheese, or peanut butter on crackers.
- Occasional sweets: fruit snacks, pudding cups, or small candy bars if we had them around the house.

After school I remember coming home very hungry and grabbing whatever was easiest—usually something crunchy and salty like chips or something sweet like cookies. I didn’t really think of snacks in terms of nutrition; it was more about taste and convenience.

As a child, I drank a lot of:
- Milk: mostly 2% cow’s milk with breakfast and sometimes at dinner.
- Fruit juice: orange juice in the morning, apple juice or grape juice during the day. These were often full-sugar juices, not watered down.
- Water: usually from the tap, but I probably didn’t drink as much water as I do now.
- Soda: not every day, but a few times a week—cola, lemon-lime soda, or root beer, especially on weekends or with pizza.
- Sports drinks: occasionally after soccer games or practices, like Gatorade.

We didn’t really have unsweetened tea or coffee for me as a kid. Most of my drinks were either milk, juice, or soda, with water when I was thirsty or playing outside.
food_information.what_is_your_food_like_these_days_breakf:
These days, I try to have a more balanced breakfast that includes protein, fiber, and some healthy fats, although I still have rushed mornings where I grab something quick. A typical week might look like:
- Oatmeal: rolled oats cooked with water or oat milk, topped with sliced banana or berries, a spoonful of peanut butter or almond butter, and a sprinkle of chia seeds or cinnamon.
- Greek yogurt bowl: plain or lightly sweetened Greek yogurt with a handful of granola, fresh fruit (like blueberries or strawberries), and sometimes a drizzle of honey.
- Eggs and toast: 1–2 scrambled or fried eggs with whole grain toast, plus avocado if I have it. On busier mornings, I might just do a hard-boiled egg and a piece of fruit.
- Smoothies: a blend of frozen berries, spinach, a half banana, protein powder (plant-based vanilla), and oat milk or water. This is my go-to when I’m running late but still want something filling.

I drink coffee every morning—usually one medium mug of coffee with a splash of oat milk and a small amount of sugar or flavored creamer. On workdays, I try to eat breakfast within an hour of waking up to help with energy and to avoid strong mid-morning cravings for pastries or snacks at the office.
food_information.what_is_your_food_like_these_days_lunch:
For lunch these days, I aim for something relatively simple and balanced that I can either pack from home or assemble quickly. Common lunches include:
- Salad bowls: mixed greens or spinach topped with grilled chicken or canned chickpeas, cherry tomatoes, cucumber, shredded carrots, a small amount of shredded cheese or feta, nuts or seeds (like sunflower seeds), and a vinaigrette dressing. Sometimes I add quinoa or brown rice for extra carbs.
- Leftovers: whatever we had for dinner the night before, such as baked chicken with roasted vegetables and rice, or a portion of chili or soup.
- Sandwiches or wraps: turkey and cheese with lettuce, tomato, and mustard on whole wheat bread or in a whole grain wrap, plus a side of baby carrots, hummus, and a piece of fruit.
- Quick options: if I’m short on time, I’ll have a piece of whole grain toast with avocado and a boiled egg, or a microwaveable brown rice and veggie bowl with some added beans or tofu.

I generally drink water with lunch, occasionally flavored sparkling water. I try not to skip lunch because I’ve noticed that if I do, I end up overeating or craving sugary snacks in the late afternoon.
food_information.what_is_your_food_like_these_days_dinner:
Dinner these days varies but I’m trying to focus on more vegetables, lean proteins, and whole grains while still making meals that my family will actually eat. A typical week of dinners might include:
- Baked or grilled chicken: served with roasted vegetables (broccoli, Brussels sprouts, carrots, or sweet potatoes) and a side of quinoa or brown rice.
- Stir-fry: tofu or chicken stir-fried with mixed vegetables (bell peppers, snap peas, carrots, onions) in a light soy-ginger sauce, served over brown rice or rice noodles.
- Taco night: ground turkey or black bean tacos in corn or whole wheat tortillas, topped with lettuce, tomato, avocado, salsa, and a little shredded cheese. Sometimes we make a taco salad instead of using tortillas.
- Pasta: whole wheat or lentil pasta with tomato-based sauce, lots of vegetables (zucchini, mushrooms, spinach), and either turkey meatballs or white beans for protein. I try to watch portion sizes and pair it with a side salad.
- Soup or chili: a big pot of vegetable and bean soup, lentil soup, or turkey chili that we can eat for a couple of nights. Served with a slice of whole grain bread or a small piece of cornbread.
- Takeout once a week: usually something like sushi, Thai food (vegetable curry with rice), or occasionally pizza. When we do pizza, I try to add a big side salad and limit myself to 2–3 slices instead of mindlessly eating more.

I still enjoy comfort foods, but I’m more conscious now about adding vegetables and not overeating late at night. We usually eat dinner between 6:30 and 7:30 p.m.
food_information.what_is_your_food_like_these_days_snacks:
My snacking habits are a mix of intentional, planned snacks and more emotional or stress-related snacking, especially in the late afternoon and evening. I try to keep healthier options on hand, but I do still struggle with cravings for sweets and crunchy snacks.

Typical daytime snacks:
- Fresh fruit: apples, bananas, grapes, or berries.
- Nuts: a small handful of almonds or mixed nuts (I try to portion these out because it’s easy to overdo it).
- Veggies and hummus: baby carrots, cucumber slices, or bell pepper strips with hummus.
- Greek yogurt or cottage cheese: sometimes with fruit or a drizzle of honey.

Evening or “craving” snacks:
- Dark chocolate: a few squares after dinner; sometimes this stays moderate, other times I end up going back for more.
- Popcorn: air-popped with a little olive oil and salt, or a lightly buttered microwave popcorn.
- Crackers or chips: especially if I’m stressed or watching TV—tortilla chips with salsa, pita chips, or whatever is in the pantry. This is an area where I tend to overeat if I’m not paying attention.
- Occasional sweets: cookies or ice cream, especially on weekends or if I’ve had a hard day.

I’ve noticed that if I skip meals or under-eat earlier in the day, I’m much more likely to over-snack at night. I’m working on pausing before I snack in the evening and asking myself if I’m actually hungry, thirsty, tired, or just trying to unwind emotionally.
food_information.what_is_your_food_like_these_days_liquid:
Currently, I’m much more conscious about staying hydrated and reducing sugary drinks than I was when I was younger.

Typical daily liquids:
- Water: I aim for around 60–70 ounces per day. I keep a 24-ounce reusable water bottle at my desk and try to refill it 2–3 times. I sometimes add a squeeze of lemon or a few cucumber slices for flavor.
- Coffee: 1–2 cups in the morning, usually with oat milk and a small amount of sugar or flavored creamer. I rarely drink coffee after 2 p.m. because it interferes with my sleep.
- Herbal tea: chamomile, peppermint, or a caffeine-free blend in the evening a few nights a week to help me wind down.
- Sparkling water: flavored (unsweetened) seltzer like lime or berry, usually one can in the afternoon instead of soda.

Occasional drinks:
- Alcohol: 1–3 drinks per week on average, usually a glass of red wine with dinner or a hard seltzer on the weekend. I’ve noticed even small amounts can affect my sleep, so I’m trying to be more mindful.
- Juice: very rarely, maybe a small glass of orange juice on a weekend morning or a splash of juice in sparkling water.
- Soda: not a daily habit anymore; maybe 1–2 times per month if we order pizza or go to a movie.

Overall, I focus mainly on water, coffee in the morning, and herbal tea or sparkling water later in the day, with much less emphasis on sugary drinks than in my childhood.
